skill.name: terraform-aws-modules
skill.description: Terraform module creation for AWS — reusable modules, state management, and HCL best practices. Use when building or reviewing Terraform AWS infrastructure.
skill.summary: Build and review reusable Terraform modules for AWS infrastructure.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick, network, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found Markdown formatting and Terraform examples rather than executable code. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, credential theft, or malicious automation was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the cited lines are Markdown inline code, command names, and Terraform concepts. No executable script or command runner is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is plain Markdown guidance and inline code formatting. There is no Ruby, shell script, subprocess call, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Terraform Examples Reported as External Commands
low_findings.description: The HCL example blocks describe Terraform variables, AWS VPC resources, outputs, and an S3 backend. They are documentation examples, not code that executes during skill use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content is fenced HCL in a Markdown file and has no runtime wrapper. It cannot execute unless a user separately copies it into Terraform.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Private CIDR Reported as Hardcoded IP Address
low_findings.description: The flagged value is the default private CIDR range for a sample VPC variable. It is a common Terraform example and is not a remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line defines an example CIDR block inside a Terraform variable. It does not create an outbound network connection or identify an exfiltration target.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Indicator Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The flagged lines describe Terraform, state management, and remote state strategy. No weak algorithm name, cryptographic implementation, or unsafe hashing behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto API or weak algorithm usage on the cited lines. The only security-related term is Terraform state management.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Indicator Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The cited lines discuss provider version pinning, DNS hostname settings, and Terraform output values. They do not gather host details or inspect the local system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Terraform documentation and do not contain commands such as uname, whoami, env collection, or filesystem discovery.

content.value_statement: Terraform AWS modules are hard to keep reusable, secure, and consistent across teams. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through module structure, state design, and HCL review.
actual_capabilities: Guides reusable AWS Terraform module structure with variables, outputs, main resources, and provider versions.
Reviews Terraform code for HCL conventions, stable resource identity, tagging, and state handling.
Explains remote state patterns using S3 backend storage, DynamoDB locking, and encryption.
Helps plan migrations from manual AWS setup or CloudFormation to Terraform modules.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for Terraform state lock recovery.
limitations: Focused on AWS Terraform, not AWS CDK, Pulumi, or CloudFormation authoring.
Does not validate live AWS accounts or inspect deployed infrastructure.
Does not replace security review of provider permissions, IAM policies, or production plans.
Examples are concise and need adaptation before production deployment.

use_cases.title: Create a Shared VPC Module
use_cases.description: Design a reusable VPC module with clear inputs, outputs, provider constraints, and tagging conventions.

use_cases.title: Review Terraform Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check AWS Terraform changes for stable resource addressing, version pinning, formatting, validation, and state safety.

use_cases.title: Plan Remote State Standards
use_cases.description: Define S3 backend storage, DynamoDB locking, encryption, and workspace practices for team Terraform usage.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a reusable Terraform module for an AWS VPC. Include the files I should create, key variables, outputs, and provider version guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Review Module Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Terraform AWS module structure for maintainability. Focus on variables, outputs, provider constraints, tags, and whether resource identity is stable.

prompt_templates.title: Design Team State Management
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a remote state strategy for a team using Terraform on AWS. Cover S3 backend layout, DynamoDB locking, encryption, environments, and review practices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration to Modules
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me migrate existing AWS infrastructure into reusable Terraform modules. Identify module boundaries, state migration risks, validation steps, and rollout sequencing.

output_examples.input: Review this AWS Terraform module plan for production readiness.
output: A structured review of module files, variables, outputs, provider constraints, tags, and state safety.
A list of specific changes to improve maintainability and reduce accidental resource replacement.
A short release checklist covering formatting, validation, plan review, and state backup.

output_examples.input: Help design remote state for Terraform on AWS.
output: A recommended S3 backend layout for environments and teams.
DynamoDB locking guidance with encryption and access control considerations.
Operational notes for state lock recovery and pull request review.

best_practices: Use explicit provider version constraints and run formatting and validation before review.
Prefer for_each for resources that need stable identity across changes.
Store team state remotely with locking, encryption, and restricted access.
anti_patterns: Do not commit Terraform state files or secrets to version control.
Do not use count for resources where index changes can replace important infrastructure.
Do not treat sample CIDR ranges, names, or backend keys as production-ready defaults.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete production modules?
faq.answer: It provides structure, review guidance, and examples. Production modules still need account-specific inputs, testing, and security review.

faq.question: Can it review existing Terraform code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review AWS Terraform modules for structure, HCL practices, state usage, tags, and common maintainability issues.

faq.question: Does it support providers beyond AWS?
faq.answer: No. The skill is written for Terraform modules that manage AWS infrastructure.

faq.question: Can it run terraform commands for me?
faq.answer: No. It gives guidance about commands such as formatting and validation, but the skill file contains no executable automation.

faq.question: Does it handle remote state design?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers S3 backend storage, DynamoDB locking, encryption, and environment organization.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples and Markdown formatting, with no confirmed malicious behavior.
